text.title: Release Planning Antipatterns
text.summary: Common failure modes in release planning with root causes and concrete mitigations for the PM role.

antipattern.description: Train-the-monster: monthly release trains accumulate too much scope; one feature slips and the whole train slips.
antipattern.reason: Large trains make pull-or-postpone tradeoffs painful at the last minute. Counter: smaller trains with feature flags decouple readiness from ship date.

antipattern.description: Communication-plan inflation: PM builds a matrix of every audience × every channel × every revision for every release.
antipattern.reason: Most rows produce zero customer behavior change. Cut to channels where engagement can be measured; delete the rest.

antipattern.description: Rollback theater: every plan documents "Rollback Plan: revert PR" without ever drilling it in staging.
antipattern.reason: First real rollback fails and discredits the PM. Drill rollback in staging at least quarterly with the on-call who would execute it.

antipattern.description: Release notes written at release start and edited manually through the cycle rather than generated from merged-PR labels at deploy time.
antipattern.reason: Manual notes drift from reality as PRs land and cuts happen. Stale notes ship inaccurate and surface as customer trust incidents.

antipattern.description: PM as deploy gate: PM treats the release plan as a veto over engineering deploy mechanics.
antipattern.reason: Engineering owns deploy mechanics. The PM owns external readiness. Conflating the two makes the PM the bottleneck and erodes cross-functional trust.

antipattern.description: Hidden dependencies: a feature depends on a back-office migration or vendor contract renewal not tracked in the engineering tracker.
antipattern.reason: Dependencies not in the manifest look green but are actually red. Mandate a cross-team dependencies section with a named owner per row.

antipattern.description: Breaking-change detection delegated entirely to LLM agents reading PR titles.
antipattern.reason: Agents miss implicit breaking changes (default value flipped, undocumented header now required). Breaking-change rows must be human-asserted.

antipattern.description: Cycle time tracked only as "ship-date hit-rate" rather than median feature commit-to-live time.
antipattern.reason: Ship-date hit-rate hides the truth: teams ship smaller things slower over time. Cycle time trend is the leading indicator of release health.
